# Functions  {.unnumbered}

## Python's batteries {.nostretch}

![](images/bateries-included.png){fig-align="center" width=55%}

<p font-size=55pt align="center">
**Python comes with "batteries included".**
</p>

The standard library provides built-in support for lots of common tasks, including the ability to interact with the operating system, open, read and write files, perform numerical & mathematical operations, and many more.

## Fundamentals

Let's take a look at a simple example:

```{python}
# | echo: True
print("Hello World")
```

*Functions* take the form:

<p font-size=55pt align="center">
```function_name(arguments)``` 
</p>

where the ```arguments``` are the elements we want to apply the function to. Different functions require a different number and type of arguments.

::: {.callout-important}
### When using a function you must always use parentheses, even if they’re empty, so that Python knows a function is being called.
:::

## Common examples

In addition to ```print```, there are other built-in functions that behave intuitively.


Measuring the length of something:
```{python}
#| echo: True
len('ABCDEFG')
```


Finding the minimum of a list of values:
```{python}
#| echo: True
min(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
```

Finding the maximum of a list of values:
```{python}
#| echo: True
max(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
```

:::{.callout-note}
### Every function takes a different number of arguments, and every function returns *something*.
:::

<!-- Every function call produces some result.
If the function doesn’t have a useful result to return, it usually returns the special value None. None is a Python object that stands in anytime there is no value. -->

## Default arguments

Functions may have default values for some arguments. For example, ```round``` will round off a floating-point number:

```{python}
#| echo: True
round(3.3333333333, 4)
```


By default, it will round to zero decimal places if the second argument (the number of decimal places) is not provided.

```{python}
#| echo: True
round(3.3333333333)
```

:::{.callout-important}
### The position of the arguments here determines what is the number to be rounded, and what is the number of places to be rounded to. This is called providing *positional arguments*, and can lead to some hard-to-track bugs!
:::

## Help!

::: {.callout-tip}
### How do you find out what arguments, and in what order, different built-in functions require? 
:::

Of course, you could always consult the [official documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html)

There is also a built-in ```help``` function that gives access to this info in your editor!
```{python}
#| echo: True
help(max)
```

::: {.callout-note}
### In jupyter, you can also add a ```?``` after a function name.
:::

<!-- --- -->

## In practice

::: {.callout-caution}

### Exercises

1.1 Printing fundamentals

1.2 Using ```min()``` and ```max()``` to assess values

1.3 Explore the effect of default arguments and improperly formatted function calls

:::
